new_principle_classes.label: Competence Principle
new_principle_classes.definition: The value that a professional performs services only in areas of their competence and undertakes assignments only when qualified by education or experience in the specific technical fields involved.
text_references: perform services only in areas of their competence
undertake assignments only when qualified by education or experience in the specific technical fields involved
new_principle_classes.source_text: Fundamental Canon I.2 states that engineers "perform services only in areas of their competence" and Code section II.2.a states that engineers must "undertake assignments only when qualified by education or experience in the specific technical fields involved."

match_decision.reasoning: The discussion invokes Fundamental Canon I.2 competence, which is exactly the existing Competence Principle, the professional virtue principle requiring maintenance and application of professional knowledge and skill within areas of competence.

new_principle_classes.label: Confidentiality Principle
new_principle_classes.definition: The relational value protecting client information, under which confidential client information may only be shared with the prior consent of the client.
text_references: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client.
new_principle_classes.source_text: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client. While careful review and checking of AI-generated content was consistent for ethical use, this does not end the inquiry into Engineer A's actions.

match_decision.reasoning: The discussion invokes the client confidentiality value under Code II.1.c, which is the same concept as the existing Confidentiality Principle protecting client information and maintaining trust in professional relationships.

new_principle_classes.label: Attribution and Credit Principle
new_principle_classes.definition: The value that a professional gives credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due, recognizes the proprietary interests of others, and includes appropriate citations of documents of technical authority.
text_references: give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due
[e]ngineers shall give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due, and will recognize the proprietary interests of others.
new_principle_classes.source_text: Per Code section III.9, engineers are required to "give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due," so Engineer A's ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations.

match_decision.reasoning: The Code III.9 credit-and-citation value invoked here is the same concept as the previously extracted Attribution and Credit Principle.

extensional_examples: BER Case 98-3 emphasized that engineers must acknowledge significant contributions by others

new_principle_classes.label: Professional Judgment Primacy Principle
new_principle_classes.definition: The value that a tool or technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgment; outputs of tools must be considered, challenged, and understood before acceptance by the responsible engineer.
text_references: technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement
Recommendations from the program or intern should not be blindly accepted, they should be considered and challenged and the resulting outputs should be understood.
new_principle_classes.source_text: The BER notes that in BER Case 98-3 , the BER stated that technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement. Much like directing an engineering intern to solve a problem, responsible use of AI requires an engineer to outline solution guidelines and constraints.

match_decision.reasoning: The value that technology must not substitute for engineering judgment is exactly the existing Professional Judgment Primacy Principle, that a tool or technology does not substitute for the professional judgment and competence the work requires.

extensional_examples: BER Case 98-3, where the BER stated that technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement

text_references: Here, Engineer A, as an experienced environmental engineer, was competent to analyze groundwater monitoring data and assess contaminant risks.
it is the BER’s view that under the facts, unlike the situation of BER Case 98-3 , Engineer A is not incompetent
principle_individuals.source_text: Fundamental Canon I.2 states that engineers "perform services only in areas of their competence" and Code section II.2.a states that engineers must "undertake assignments only when qualified by education or experience in the specific technical fields involved." Here, Engineer A, as an experienced environmental engineer, was competent to analyze groundwater monitoring data and assess contaminant risks.

match_decision.reasoning: This invocation instantiates the existing Competence Principle class.

principle_individuals.concrete_expression: The Board finds Engineer A, as an experienced environmental engineer with several years of experience and strong technical expertise, was competent to analyze groundwater monitoring data and assess contaminant risks, and holds that Engineer A is not incompetent.
applied_to: Engineer A's qualification to analyze groundwater monitoring data and assess contaminant risks
the Board's determination that Engineer A is not incompetent despite operating without Engineer B's help
principle_individuals.interpretation: In this case competence requires that the engineer be qualified by education or experience for the specific technical fields involved; the Board finds this satisfied for Engineer A, while noting Engineer A was operating in a compromised manner without Engineer B's oversight.

text_references: Per Code section II.1.c, confidential information can only be shared with prior consent of the Client.
When Engineer A uploaded Client W’s information into the AI open-source interface, this was tantamount to placing the Client’s private information in the public domain.
principle_individuals.source_text: When Engineer A uploaded Client W's information into the AI open-source interface, this was tantamount to placing the Client's private information in the public domain. The facts here do not indicate Engineer A obtained permission from Client W to use the private information in the public domain.

match_decision.reasoning: This invocation instantiates the existing Confidentiality Principle class.

principle_individuals.concrete_expression: The Board treats Engineer A's upload of Client W's information into the AI open-source interface as tantamount to placing the client's private information in the public domain, without any indicated permission from Client W.
applied_to: Engineer A's upload of Client W's private information into the AI open-source interface without prior consent
principle_individuals.interpretation: In this case confidentiality requires prior client consent before client information is shared; uploading it into an open-source AI interface without permission places it in the public domain and falls short of the value.

principle_individuals.identifier: Attribution and Credit for AI Work
text_references: Per Code section III.9, engineers are required to “give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due,” so Engineer A’s ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations.
AI, while not a human contributor, fundamentally shaped the report and design documents, warranting disclosure under Code section III.9
principle_individuals.source_text: Similarly, the facts do not indicate the AI-generated report included citations of pertinent documents of technical authority. Per Code section III.9, engineers are required to "give credit for engineering work to those to whom credit is due," so Engineer A's ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations.

match_decision.reasoning: This invocation instantiates the previously extracted Attribution and Credit Principle class.

principle_individuals.concrete_expression: The AI-generated report lacked citations of pertinent documents of technical authority, and the Board holds that ethical use of the AI software would need to include appropriate citations, with AI's fundamental shaping of the report and design documents warranting disclosure under Code III.9.
applied_to: the missing citations of documents of technical authority in the AI-generated report
disclosure of AI's fundamental contribution to the report and design documents
principle_individuals.interpretation: In this case giving credit requires appropriate citations of the technical authorities relied on and warrants disclosure that AI fundamentally shaped the report and design documents, even though AI is not a human contributor.

text_references: the BER stated that technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement
Recommendations from the program or intern should not be blindly accepted, they should be considered and challenged and the resulting outputs should be understood.
These are steps that, in this case, Engineer A chose not to follow.
principle_individuals.source_text: The BER notes that in BER Case 98-3 , the BER stated that technology must not replace or be used as a substitute for engineering judgement. Much like directing an engineering intern to solve a problem, responsible use of AI requires an engineer to outline solution guidelines and constraints. Recommendations from the program or intern should not be blindly accepted, they should be considered and challenged and the resulting outputs should be understood. Only after the engineer in Responsible Charge has satisfied themselves that the proposed solution is in accordance with their own and professional standards should the design/report be accepted. These are steps that, in this case, Engineer A chose not to follow.

match_decision.reasoning: This invocation instantiates the existing Professional Judgment Primacy Principle class.

principle_individuals.concrete_expression: The Board applies the value that technology must not substitute for engineering judgment, holding that responsible use of AI requires outlining solution guidelines and constraints, challenging and understanding the outputs, and accepting the design or report only after the engineer is satisfied it meets professional standards, steps Engineer A chose not to follow.
applied_to: Engineer A's reliance on the AI-generated plans and specifications without proper oversight
acceptance of AI-generated design documents and the report
principle_individuals.interpretation: In this case the value requires that AI recommendations not be blindly accepted; the engineer must consider and challenge them, understand the resulting outputs, and accept the work only after satisfying themselves it accords with their own and professional standards.
